content.text: LangGraph provides four ways to observe graph execution progressively, ranging from coarse state snapshots to individual LLM tokens. Choosing the right streaming mode depends on whether you need to display live output, debug node interactions, build reactive UIs, or monitor agent decisions.

attrs.alt: LangGraph streaming modes: values, updates, debug, and astream_events with their characteristics

content.text: Every time a node finishes, the complete current state is yielded. This is the simplest mode: each yielded value is the state dict after that node ran. Use it when you need a complete snapshot at every step and the state is small enough to send over the wire.

yields only what each node changed — a dict with one key (the node name) mapping to the node's return value. This is the most bandwidth-efficient mode and the most useful for building reactive UIs that show per-node progress.

for step in app.stream(initial_state, stream_mode="updates"):
    node_name, delta = next(iter(step.items()))
    print(f"[{node_name}] changed: {list(delta.keys())}")
    if "messages" in delta:
        new_msg = delta["messages"][-1]
        print(f"  New message: {str(new_msg.content)[:80]}")

# Example output:
# [agent]  changed: ['messages']
#   New message: I'll search for that information.
# [tools]  changed: ['messages']
#   New message: <tool result>
# [agent]  changed: ['messages']
#   New message: Based on the search, here is the answer...
minimises the payload — you only send the diff, not the full accumulated state. For chat UIs, extract the latest message from the delta and append it to the conversation thread.
mode yields low-level events for every internal step: task starts, task results, checkpoints, and errors. Each event has a
field. Use this for deep debugging, not for production UI.
is an async generator that emits fine-grained events for every operation in the graph: LLM token streams, tool calls, chain starts and ends. Each event has
fields. This is the right choice for showing token-by-token LLM output to users.

import asyncio
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage

async def stream_to_user(query: str):
    async for event in app.astream_events(
        {"messages": [HumanMessage(content=query)]},
        version="v2",
    ):
        kind = event["event"]
        name = event.get("name", "")

        if kind == "on_chat_model_stream":
            # Individual token from an LLM
            chunk = event["data"]["chunk"]
            if chunk.content:
                print(chunk.content, end="", flush=True)

        elif kind == "on_tool_start":
            tool_input = event["data"].get("input", {})
            print(f"\n[calling {name}({tool_input})]\n")

        elif kind == "on_tool_end":
            result = event["data"].get("output", "")
            print(f"[{name} returned: {str(result)[:60]}]")

asyncio.run(stream_to_user("What is the capital of France?"))
to narrow the event stream to only what you need.

# Only stream events from the "agent" node
async for event in app.astream_events(
    initial_state,
    version="v2",
    include_names=["agent"],           # only events from this node
):
    if event["event"] == "on_chat_model_stream":
        print(event["data"]["chunk"].content, end="", flush=True)

# Only LLM token events (ignores tool calls, chain events, etc.)
async for event in app.astream_events(
    initial_state,
    version="v2",
    include_types=["chat_model"],      # only LLM events
):
    if event["event"] == "on_chat_model_stream":
        print(event["data"]["chunk"].content, end="", flush=True)
Server-Sent Events (SSE) is the standard protocol for pushing streamed graph output to a browser. Use FastAPI's
to send tokens as they are generated.

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
import json

api = FastAPI()

@api.post("/chat/stream")
async def chat_stream(query: str):
    async def event_generator():
        async for event in app.astream_events(
            {"messages": [HumanMessage(content=query)]},
            version="v2",
        ):
            if event["event"] == "on_chat_model_stream":
                chunk = event["data"]["chunk"]
                if chunk.content:
                    data = json.dumps({"token": chunk.content})
                    yield f"data: {data}\n\n"
        yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"

    return StreamingResponse(event_generator(), media_type="text/event-stream")
You can combine modes by passing a list to
— useful when you need both node-level updates and debug events in the same loop.
Async Streaming with stream and astream
All streaming methods have async equivalents. Use
for the events API. Both are async generators — iterate them with

import asyncio
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver

app = graph.compile(checkpointer=MemorySaver())

async def run_async():
    config = {"configurable": {"thread_id": "async-1"}}

    # Async stream_mode="updates"
    async for step in app.astream(initial_state, config=config, stream_mode="updates"):
        node, delta = next(iter(step.items()))
        print(f"[{node}] {list(delta.keys())}")

asyncio.run(run_async())
If you need all intermediate steps for logging, testing, or replay, collect the stream into a list. For
this gives you a complete audit trail of every node's output in order.

# Collect all steps — useful for tests and replay
all_steps = list(app.stream(initial_state, stream_mode="updates"))
node_sequence = [list(s.keys())[0] for s in all_steps]
print("Execution path:", " -> ".join(node_sequence))

# Verify expected path in a test
assert "agent" in node_sequence
assert node_sequence.index("tools") > node_sequence.index("agent")

# Replay: find what each node produced
for step in all_steps:
    node, delta = next(iter(step.items()))
    if node == "agent" and "messages" in delta:
        last = delta["messages"][-1]
        print(f"Agent said: {last.content[:80]}")
When a subgraph is used as a node, its internal streaming events are nested under the subgraph's namespace in
to also receive delta events from within subgraph nodes.
is set, each item in the stream is a
tuple. The namespace is a tuple of node names tracing the call stack. An empty namespace means the event is from the outermost graph.
Test streaming by collecting all emitted steps and asserting the execution sequence or per-node outputs. A mock LLM with predictable output makes tests deterministic — no real API calls needed.
stream_mode='values' yields the full state dict after each node — simplest but highest payload.
stream_mode='updates' yields only the node name and its state delta — preferred for production UIs.
stream_mode='debug' emits internal task, result, and checkpoint events — useful for debugging.
astream_events(version='v2') is async and emits the finest granularity: individual LLM tokens via on_chat_model_stream.
Filter astream_events with include_names, include_types, or include_tags to narrow the event stream.
Pair astream_events with FastAPI StreamingResponse and SSE to stream token-by-token output to browsers.
